model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Alabama's elite offense, ranked 3rd nationally with an AdjO of 129.0, should be the decisive factor against a Texas Tech team that is reeling without Big 12 Player of the Year JT Toppin, who tore his ACL in mid-February and whose absence has been increasingly devastating—culminating in a 75-53 blowout loss to Iowa State in the Big 12 Tournament where the Red Raiders shot just 22.2% in the second half. While Alabama is also diminished by the suspension of second-leading scorer Aden Holloway following a felony drug arrest, the Crimson Tide's remaining offensive firepower with Labaron Philon and Latrell Wrightsell Jr. still vastly exceeds what Texas Tech can generate without their do-it-all star, and Alabama's roughly 4-point offensive efficiency advantage should overwhelm Texas Tech's solid but not elite defense in a game between two compromised rosters where the team with the higher offensive ceiling holds the edge.

reasoningStep.title: Alabama vs Texas Tech: Battle of Diminished Rosters Favors Bama's Offensive Firepower
reasoningStep.summary: Both teams enter with significant personnel losses—Texas Tech without Big 12 POY JT Toppin (torn ACL) and Alabama without suspended second-leading scorer Aden Holloway—but Alabama's remaining offensive ceiling is substantially higher. Texas Tech's late-season collapse without Toppin (including a 75-53 blowout loss in the Big 12 Tournament) suggests they cannot generate enough offense to keep pace with Alabama's #3-ranked attack, even with their strong defensive identity.
evidence: Alabama AdjO 129.0 (3rd nationally) vs Texas Tech AdjO 125.1 (12th) - Alabama has a roughly 4-point offensive efficiency advantage

Texas Tech lost JT Toppin (Big 12 POY, leading scorer/rebounder/shot-blocker) to torn ACL; went 3-0 initially but then collapsed, losing to BYU and getting blown out 75-53 by Iowa State in Big 12 Tournament
Alabama lost Aden Holloway (2nd-leading scorer, top 3-point shooter) to suspension but retains Labaron Philon and Latrell Wrightsell Jr. as primary scorers
KenPom efficiency margins are close (Alabama +25.64 vs Texas Tech +25.19) but Alabama's offensive advantage is larger than Texas Tech's defensive advantage
Texas Tech's defense (AdjD 98.8-99.9, 27th) is strong but not elite enough to fully contain Alabama's explosive attack, especially without Toppin's rim protection

toolCalls.summary: Fetched https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/college/red-raiders/2026/03/08/texas-tech-basketball-loss-jt-toppin-knee-injury-big-12-player-year-award-repeat-byu-loss/88893929007/ and extracted 3000 characters of text.

result.text: Texas Tech basketball feeling impact of JT Toppin's absence more and more Skip to main content Home NCAAB NCAAW MLB Women's Sports NFL NBA NCAAF WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Olympics Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers How Texas Tech basketball's loss to BYU should cement JT Toppin as Big 12 POY Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal Despite the team's 3-0 record immediately after losing JT Toppin to a season-ending ACL tear , the Texas Tech basketball team was never better without the reigning Big 12 player of the year on the floor. Timing is the biggest issue of Toppin's absence. Being so close to March Madness, with the NCAA Tournament on the horizon, the Red Raiders have had to adjust everything from how they start their offense to how they cover ball screens on defense, and had five regular-season games to do it before the postseason arrived. The first three games were a breath of fresh air, showing what other players on the team are capable of while giving opponents a new look from their typical scouting report of "find Toppin, try to limit him." Kansas State and Cincinnati didn't have the luxury of advance scouting, and Iowa State probably felt confident it would get the job done at home. The Cyclones were wrong. TCU and BYU, meanwhile, had a three-game run-up to see what this version of the Red Raiders looks like. And the secret is out: They're not physically tough and can be pushed around. Christian Anderson has a tendency to shy away the paint, even if Texas Tech needs something going to the basket or even if the layup is wide open to take. He started turning it over much more without Toppin as the safety valve. Luke Bamgboye and Josiah Moseley have not been anywhere as lethal with the ball in their hands. BYU decided not to trap Anderson, instead switching every ball screen — real or bluffed — and putting the 6-foot-3 point guard into positions to be a 1-on-1 scorer. Anderson made them pay in the first half but went quiet until the final few minutes as Texas Tech was forging a comeback attempt. Nobody's going to point the finger at Anderson (23 points, 9 assists) for the 82-76 loss to the Cougars on Saturday, March 7, nor should they. That loss, like the TCU setback, can be attributed to one glaring issue. The frontcourt has gone soft. It wasn't the most physical team in the country even with Toppin, and now that he's gone there's little resistance in the paint. Texas Tech was outscored 48-14 in the paint, giving up 20 offense rebounds to BYU, which turned them into another 20 points. Same song and dance as the TCU game. Push came to shove, and the Red Raider

toolCalls.summary: Fetched https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/college/sec/2026/03/16/can-alabama-basketball-survive-march-madness-without-aden-holloway/89183298007/ and extracted 3000 characters of text.

result.text: Can Alabama basketball survive March Madness without Aden Holloway? Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Spirits were low for Alabama basketball coming into the NCAA Tournament after an early exit from the SEC tourney in Nashville. Now, the Crimson Tide carries more heartbreak to Tampa as it stares down March Madness without its top three-point shooter. On Monday, March 16, Alabama guard Aden Holloway was arrested for first-degree possession of marijuana and failure to affix a tax stamp and was transported to the Tuscaloosa County Jail. The University of Alabama has yet to release a statement on Holloway, or his status. However, facing two felony charges, it is hard to see a world in which the junior returns to competition. Not with the Crimson Tide, but possibly ever again. REQUIRED READING: What failure to affix tax stamp means for Alabama basketball's Aden Holloway GOODBREAD: Why Alabama's Nate Oats should suspend Aden Holloway Holloway's decisions have impacted more than just himself, though. The question is no longer whether Alabama can win a national championship in the face of adversity. Instead, it's if the Crimson Tide can survive even one game without one of the anchors to its nationally-acclaimed backcourt. Prior to his arrest, Holloway was averaging 16.8 points per game. Converting nearly 44% of attempts, he was the nation's 11th best shooter from the arc and ranked third in the SEC. He shot 48% from the field and also had 3.8 assists per game. Alabama has competed without Holloway before — as he dealt with a nagging wrist injury throughout non-conference play. Considering the Crimson Tide's strength-of-schedule, the fact that Alabama has done it without Holloway at 100% could suggest that it isn't time to panic. Well, March is not December. Holloway missed the season opener against North Dakota. He also was sidelined for the SEC/ACC Challenge against Clemson and the next game against UTSA. Arizona marked the Auburn transfer's return, but the 96-75 loss also gave an idea of what Alabama looks like on a Holloway off-day as he went scoreless over 21 minutes. A complete domination of UTSA also made it easier to forget how the Crimson Tide crumbled in the second half against the Tigers. Holloway hasn't missed a game since Dec. 13, and Alabama couldn't have afforded for him to be on the bench against Texas A&M or on the road at Neville Arena. In those

result.text: Texas Tech basketball feeling impact of JT Toppin's absence more and more Skip to main content Home NCAAB NCAAW MLB Women's Sports NFL NBA NCAAF WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Olympics Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers How Texas Tech basketball's loss to BYU should cement JT Toppin as Big 12 POY Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal Despite the team's 3-0 record immediately after losing JT Toppin to a season-ending ACL tear , the Texas Tech basketball team was never better without the reigning Big 12 player of the year on the floor. Timing is the biggest issue of Toppin's absence. Being so close to March Madness, with the NCAA Tournament on the horizon, the Red Raiders have had to adjust everything from how they start their offense to how they cover ball screens on defense, and had five regular-season games to do it before the postseason arrived. The first three games were a breath of fresh air, showing what other players on the team are capable of while giving opponents a new look from their typical scouting report of "find Toppin, try to limit him." Kansas State and Cincinnati didn't have the luxury of advance scouting, and Iowa State probably felt confident it would get the job done at home. The Cyclones were wrong. TCU and BYU, meanwhile, had a three-game run-up to see what this version of the Red Raiders looks like. And the secret is out: They're not physically tough and can be pushed around. Christian Anderson has a tendency to shy away the paint, even if Texas Tech needs something going to the basket or even if the layup is wide open to take. He started turning it over much more without Toppin as the safety valve. Luke Bamgboye and Josiah Moseley have not been anywhere as lethal with the ball in their hands. BYU decided not to trap Anderson, instead switching every ball screen — real or bluffed — and putting the 6-foot-3 point guard into positions to be a 1-on-1 scorer. Anderson made them pay in the first half but went quiet until the final few minutes as Texas Tech was forging a comeback attempt. Nobody's going to point the finger at Anderson (23 points, 9 assists) for the 82-76 loss to the Cougars on Saturday, March 7, nor should they. That loss, like the TCU setback, can be attributed to one glaring issue. The frontcourt has gone soft. It wasn't the most physical team in the country even with Toppin, and now that he's gone there's little resistance in the paint. Texas Tech was outscored 48-14 in the paint, giving up 20 offense rebounds to BYU, which turned them into another 20 points. Same song and dance as the TCU game. Push came to shove, and the Red Raider

Can Alabama basketball survive March Madness without Aden Holloway? Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Spirits were low for Alabama basketball coming into the NCAA Tournament after an early exit from the SEC tourney in Nashville. Now, the Crimson Tide carries more heartbreak to Tampa as it stares down March Madness without its top three-point shooter. On Monday, March 16, Alabama guard Aden Holloway was arrested for first-degree possession of marijuana and failure to affix a tax stamp and was transported to the Tuscaloosa County Jail. The University of Alabama has yet to release a statement on Holloway, or his status. However, facing two felony charges, it is hard to see a world in which the junior returns to competition. Not with the Crimson Tide, but possibly ever again. REQUIRED READING: What failure to affix tax stamp means for Alabama basketball's Aden Holloway GOODBREAD: Why Alabama's Nate Oats should suspend Aden Holloway Holloway's decisions have impacted more than just himself, though. The question is no longer whether Alabama can win a national championship in the face of adversity. Instead, it's if the Crimson Tide can survive even one game without one of the anchors to its nationally-acclaimed backcourt. Prior to his arrest, Holloway was averaging 16.8 points per game. Converting nearly 44% of attempts, he was the nation's 11th best shooter from the arc and ranked third in the SEC. He shot 48% from the field and also had 3.8 assists per game. Alabama has competed without Holloway before — as he dealt with a nagging wrist injury throughout non-conference play. Considering the Crimson Tide's strength-of-schedule, the fact that Alabama has done it without Holloway at 100% could suggest that it isn't time to panic. Well, March is not December. Holloway missed the season opener against North Dakota. He also was sidelined for the SEC/ACC Challenge against Clemson and the next game against UTSA. Arizona marked the Auburn transfer's return, but the 96-75 loss also gave an idea of what Alabama looks like on a Holloway off-day as he went scoreless over 21 minutes. A complete domination of UTSA also made it easier to forget how the Crimson Tide crumbled in the second half against the Tigers. Holloway hasn't missed a game since Dec. 13, and Alabama couldn't have afforded for him to be on the bench against Texas A&M or on the road at Neville Arena. In those

modelTrace.content:
Now let me compile the analysis. Here's what I know:

**Texas Tech (5-seed)**:
- KenPom #19, Torvik #12
- AdjO: 125.1-126.3 (9th-12th), AdjD: 98.8-99.9 (27th)
- AdjEM: +25.19 (KenPom), +27.55 (Torvik)
- Record: 22-10 (Big 12)
- Missing JT Toppin (torn ACL, Big 12 POY) - season-ending
- Lost 3 of last 5 regular season games without Toppin, then lost 75-53 to Iowa State in Big 12 Tournament quarterfinals (shot 22.2% in 2nd half)
- Still have Christian Anderson (elite PG)
- Strong defense even without Toppin

**Alabama (4-seed)**:
- KenPom #16, Torvik #17
- AdjO: 129.0-129.4 (3rd), AdjD: 102.8-103.4 (67th)
- AdjEM: +25.64 (KenPom), +26.66 (Torvik)
- Record: 23-9 (SEC)
- Missing Aden Holloway (suspended, 2nd-leading scorer, top 3-point shooter) - felony drug arrest
- Early SEC Tournament exit
- Still have Labaron Philon, Latrell Wrightsell Jr.
- Elite offense (#3 nationally)

Both teams are dealing with significant personnel losses. Texas Tech lost their best player (JT Toppin, Big 12 POY) while Alabama lost their second-leading scorer (Holloway). The key difference is that Texas Tech's loss is arguably more devastating - Toppin was their leading scorer, rebounder, and shot-blocker, and the team visibly collapsed without him (75-53 blowout loss to Iowa State).

Alabama's offense is elite (#3 nationally) even without Holloway, while Texas Tech's defense (#27) is their calling card. The matchup comes down to whether Texas Tech's defense can contain Alabama's explosive offense.

Alabama has a slight edge in overall efficiency (+25.64 vs +25.19 KenPom), but the real gap is in offensive firepower - Alabama's AdjO of 129.0 vs Texas Tech's 125.1. Alabama's defense is weaker (103.4 vs 99.9), but their offensive advantage is larger.

Both teams are diminished, but Alabama's remaining roster is better equipped to absorb their loss than Texas Tech is to absorb theirs.

While Alabama is also diminished by the suspension of second-leading scorer Aden Holloway following a felony drug arrest, the Crimson Tide's remaining offensive firepower with Labaron Philon and Latrell Wrightsell Jr. still vastly exceeds what Texas Tech can generate without their do-it-all star, and Alabama's roughly 4-point offensive efficiency advantage should overwhelm Texas Tech's solid but not elite defense in a game between two compromised rosters where the team with the higher offensive ceiling holds the edge."
